text: The surgeon begins on the left side, identifying the left ureter. The left lateral peritoneal reflection is opened, with the left colon retracted medially to expose the area. The surgeon stays on the medial aspect of the peritoneal reflection to maintain the correct surgical plane.

The surgeon performs proximal dissection, enabling further traction on the lower colon for mobilization. The dissection progresses back to the descending colon, where attachments near the left pelvic brim are divided to define the proximal aspect of the presacral space.
The surgeon moves towards the splenic flexure, dividing the lateral attachments using a combination of medial retraction and careful dissection.

The surgeon approaches the splenic flexure laterally, using a combination of medial retraction to mobilize it off Girota's fascia and the left upper retroperitoneum, providing a clear view of the spleen.
The surgeon achieves full mobilization of the splenic flexure by partially mobilizing the omentum, with the splenic flexure visibly separated from surrounding structures.

The surgeon moves to the right side, revealing the pelvis with the uterus visible deep on the left. The mesentery of the small bowel is elevated, allowing visualization of the ureter beneath the peritoneum.
The surgeon elevates the area to reveal the contrast between the yellow fat and the overlying white peritoneum, initiating dissection by scoring the peritoneum in the correct anatomical plane.

The surgeon opens the lateral attachments along the peritoneal reflection, fully mobilizing the cecum and exposing the lower aspect of the right retroperitoneum. This maneuver increases tension on the lateral attachments of the ascending colon.
The surgeon continues the dissection, further mobilizing the small bowel mesentery to expose the right retroperitoneum. The ureter, vena cava, and aorta are clearly visible in the surgical field.
The patient is positioned in reverse Trendelenburg as the hepatic flexure is reached. The appendix is observed in an anomalous position, with its tip adjacent to the liver.
The surgeon divides the hepatocolic attachments using electrocautery or a harmonic scalpel, with minimal presence of significant vessels in the area. Any vessels encountered are clipped as needed.

The surgeon identifies the duodenum and mobilizes the hepatic flexure off the retroperitoneum, ensuring the duodenum is protected. The transverse colon is shown to have sufficient mobility for later exteriorization through a peri-umbilical incision.
The surgeon lyses the final attachments to the retroperitoneum, revealing the full right retroperitoneum. The surgical focus then shifts to the mobilization of the rectum.
The surgeon continues the left lateral peritoneal dissection down into the left pararectal peritoneum, revealing the fine connective tissue of the proximal presacral plane.

Using a laparoscopic approach, the surgeon visualizes the nerves in the area more clearly. The dissection is connected in the presacral plane on the right side, visible as a darker area on the peritoneum.
The surgeon scores the right pararectal peritoneum and connects the dissection in the presacral space with the previously performed dissection from the left side.

The surgeon uses a combination of posterior and lateral dissection techniques to apply tension circumferentially around the rectum, moving sequentially from one side to the other and posteriorly to maintain tension as the rectum is dissected in the correct anatomical plane.

The uterus is elevated using a fan retractor, facilitating the initial dissection in the rectovaginal septum. The surgeon lyses the anterior attachments, increasing tension on the lateral attachments to aid in further dissection.

The surgeon continues the circumferential dissection approach around the rectum, with visible smoke accumulation in the pelvis due to the mobilized colon blocking the pelvic inlet. As the dissection progresses behind the rectum, the pelvic floor becomes clearly exposed.

The surgeon mobilizes flimsy attachments to facilitate stapler deployment, applying tension on lateral attachments for clearer visualization of the pelvic floor. The dissection proceeds anteriorly, separating the posterior wall of the vagina from the rectum, with the vagina visible anteriorly in front of the rectal dissection.

The surgeon identifies the muscle of the pelvic floor on both sides, with remaining posterior attachments visible. These attachments are lysed to allow precise placement of the stapler at the pelvic floor level, facilitating division of the rectum at the same level as in open surgery.

The surgeon places an articulated stapler across the distal rectum at the level of the pelvic floor. Tension is applied to the rectum, tenting it upwards to facilitate transection precisely at the level of the levator muscles. External pressure is applied on the perineum to assist in this maneuver, even within the male pelvis.
After fully mobilizing the colon, the surgeon exteriorizes it through an extended incision around the umbilicus at the supraumbilical port site.
The surgeon extends the periumbilical incision to a length of four to six centimeters, facilitating the exteriorization of the entire colon, spanning from the terminal ileum to the transected rectum.

The surgeon divides the vessels of the mesentery extracorporeally, utilizing the incision to exteriorize the colon. This approach mimics a standard open procedure, allowing for efficient division of the mesentery without the need for multiple staplers and clipper pliers.

After the removal of the colon, the surgeon exteriorizes the small bowel through the peri-umbilical incision, running it to check for signs of proximal Crohn's disease. The incision provides a view of the ileocolic pedicle base, and the small bowel mesentery attachments are lysed up to the duodenum, which is visible through the incision.

The surgeon uses a linear stapler to create a J-pouch, visible as the stapler fires to form the pouch. The spike of the EEA stapler is gently inserted through the anus, positioned adjacent to the staple line.

The surgeon reestablishes pneumoperitoneum and brings the pouch with the anvil secured by a purse string down to the stapler. The posterior wall of the vagina is elevated before completing the stapling process.
